sound_type: quiet plop into water -- NON-VOCAL sound, the muskrat entering water from a bank, soft concussive splash, E=3, marsh habitat
ecosystem: Marsh. Freshwater marshes, wetlands, ponds, slow-moving streams, lake edges. Dense cattail and bulrush stands. First marsh species in the engine. Aquatic habitat returns.
season: year-round. Active all year. Crepuscular -- most active at dawn and dusk. Does not hibernate.

species_pair.pair_type: Third consecutive Cricetidae (83-84-85). Deer Mouse (83, varied, E=2) to Woodrat (84, rocky, E=3) to Muskrat (85, marsh, E=3). Three consecutive Cricetidae, three radically different habitats.

coordinates.description: Reference for marsh habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands, Washington. Freshwater wetlands and cattail marshes.

taxonomy_note.description: THIRD CONSECUTIVE CRICETIDAE (83-84-85). Cricetidae streak at three. Twenty-fourth mammalian entry. Twenty-second mammalian species. Twelfth Rodentia. Third Cricetidae. NEW GENUS Ondatra -- monotypic, the muskrat. NOT A RAT (Cricetidae, not Muridae).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Ondatra from the Huron/Wyandot word for the animal. zibethicus from Latin 'musky,' referring to the perineal musk glands used during breeding season. Described by Linnaeus (1766).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Ondatra -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Monotypic (one species). Third Cricetidae genus after Peromyscus (83) and Neotoma (84).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- twelfth Rodentia in the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- twenty-fourth mammalian entry. Twenty-second mammalian species.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Cricetidae's THIRD entry at degree 85. Third consecutive (83-84-85).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: NOT a vocalization. Body-water interaction sound. Broadband splash, approximately 100-3,000 Hz.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. QUIET PLOP INTO WATER. Non-vocal body-water interaction sound.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Plop: approximately 0.1-0.3 seconds. Brief concussive impact followed by ripple sounds.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single concussive impact (the plop) followed by brief ripple/splash decay. Not a repeated pattern.
song_metrics.note_types: Plop (primary, body entering water), splash (secondary, water displacement), ripple (tertiary, surface waves).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single event: plop + splash decay. Not phrased. Occurs as a discrete event when the animal enters water.
song_metrics.timbre: Soft, concussive, aquatic. The sound of a moderate body entering calm water. Not a hard splash but a quiet plop -- the animal slides or drops in rather than leaps.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Audible at moderate range (10-20 meters) in quiet marsh conditions. Distinctive once recognized. Dawn and dusk marshes are otherwise quiet, amplifying the plop.
song_metrics.tempo: Sporadic. Each plop is a single event. Multiple muskrats may produce sequential plops. Not rhythmic.
song_metrics.context: PLOP: occurs when the muskrat enters water from a bank, log, or lodge. The primary detection sound -- you hear the plop before you see the muskrat. Also produces quiet squeaks and chatters during social interactions, but the plop is the signature sound.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. The plop is a physical phenomenon, not a learned vocalization.
song_metrics.key_property: QUIET PLOP INTO WATER -- the 86th acoustic element. NON-VOCAL. The muskrat entering water from a bank. Soft concussive splash. E=3. Marsh habitat. Heard before seen.

physical_description.overall: Medium-large semi-aquatic rodent. 700-1,800 g (1.5-4 lbs). 40-65 cm total length including tail. Laterally flattened tail (the key diagnostic). Dense waterproof fur. The largest Cricetidae in the engine.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 85: (1) LATERALLY FLATTENED TAIL -- the tail is flattened side-to-side (not round like rats, not flat like beavers). The key field mark. (2) DENSE WATERPROOF FUR -- dark brown, thick underfur. (3) PARTIALLY WEBBED HIND FEET -- for swimming. (4) SMALL EARS -- nearly hidden in fur. (5) SWIMMING POSTURE -- low in the water with tail visible as a wake.
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Dense, dark brown to reddish-brown. Thick waterproof underfur with longer guard hairs. BELLY: Lighter, grayish. TAIL: Scaly, laterally flattened, nearly hairless. Used as a rudder while swimming.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Large, prominent orange incisors. Adapted for gnawing aquatic vegetation (cattails, bulrushes). Can close lips behind the incisors to gnaw underwater.
physical_description.field_id: Medium-large rodent swimming low in the water with a laterally flattened tail creating a visible wake. Dark brown, rounded profile. Often detected first by the 'plop' sound of entering water. Much smaller than beaver. Distinctive laterally flattened tail distinguishes from all other rodents.

nodes.title: Third Consecutive Cricetidae (83-84-85)
nodes.description: Deer Mouse (83) + Woodrat (84) + Muskrat (85). Cricetidae streak at three. Three radically different habitats: varied, rocky outcrop, marsh.

nodes.title: Quiet Plop into Water -- 86th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: NON-VOCAL sound. Body entering water from a bank. Soft concussive splash. E=3. The acoustic signature of the muskrat -- heard before seen.

nodes.title: First Marsh Species -- Aquatic Habitat Returns
nodes.description: First marsh species in the engine. Aquatic habitat returns after the alpine/forest mammal era. Marsh shared with Wood Duck (58) and Mallard (59).

nodes.title: Lodge Builder -- Beaver Parallel (61)
nodes.description: Builds lodges from cattails and mud. Smaller than beaver lodges. Both semi-aquatic lodge builders. Different materials, same principle.

nodes.title: Not a Rat -- Cricetidae, Not Muridae
nodes.description: Despite its name, NOT a rat. Cricetidae (New World mice/voles), not Muridae (Old World rats/mice). The 'rat' in 'muskrat' is a misnomer.

nodes.title: The Rocky Votolato Acoustic Parallel
nodes.description: Both stripped-down returners. Both E=3. Both quiet, distinctive sounds. Both defined by what they shed: Votolato sheds the band, Muskrat sheds the land.

nodes.title: Twenty-Fifth Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-five consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. CSV reliability continues.

cross_references.relationship: LODGE BUILDER PARALLEL. Both build semi-aquatic lodges. Beaver lodges from sticks and mud. Muskrat lodges from cattails and mud. Both modify aquatic habitat. Different scales, same principle.
CRICETIDAE TRIO (83-84-85). Deer Mouse (varied habitat, E=2) to Woodrat (rocky outcrop, E=3) to Muskrat (marsh, E=3). Three consecutive Cricetidae, three radically different habitats.
CRICETIDAE TRIO (83-84-85). Woodrat builds middens on rocky outcrops. Muskrat builds lodges in marshes. Both Cricetidae builders. Rock to water.
MARSH NEIGHBOR. Both inhabit marshes and ponds. Wood Duck is waterfowl (Aves). Muskrat is mammal (Mammalia). The marsh connects species across classes.
MARSH SHARED. Both inhabit marshes, ponds, wetlands. Mallard is the most common duck. Muskrat is the most common marsh mammal. Co-inhabitants of the same habitat.
